pattern: If you only ever consider the smallest positive integers in increasing order and skip a candidate exactly when its complement (the number that would pair with it to hit the forbidden sum) is already chosen, every accepted number is as cheap as it could possibly be -- so a single greedy pass builds the minimum-sum array.
hints: Try candidates 1, 2, 3, ... in order and decide, one at a time, whether each candidate is safe to add to your growing array.

A candidate is unsafe only if its 'partner' -- the value that sums with it to the forbidden target -- is already sitting in your array; keep a set of chosen values so you can check that in one lookup.

Because you always try the smallest untested integer next and only reject it for a concrete, checkable reason, the first n integers that survive this filter are guaranteed to give the smallest possible total -- no need to search combinations or backtrack.

edge_inputs.description: largest n and k both at the cap
largest n with the smallest k
smallest n with the largest k
k forces exactly one early skip (candidate 3 is blocked by 1)
k forces several consecutive skips before jumping past k

stress.description: random n and k within the full allowed range, run repeatedly for coverage
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, 50)
    k = rng.randint(1, 50)
    return [n, k]
